problem:
Exercise 1.1
Let $\alpha,\beta\in\mathbf{R}$ satisfy $\alpha\le\beta$, and for each $a=(a_1,\dots,a_k)\in\mathbf{R}^k$ define the polynomial
\[
p(t)=a_1+a_2t+\cdots+a_kt^{k-1},\qquad t\in\mathbf{R}.
\]
Consider the set
\[
\left\{a\in\mathbf{R}^k\mid p(0)=1,\ \ |p(t)|\le 1\ \text{for all } t\in[\alpha,\beta]\right\}.
\]
Is this set convex?

Exercise 1.2
\emph{Set distributive characterization of convexity} [Rockafellar]. Let $C\subseteq \mathbf{R}^n$ be a nonempty set. For $\gamma\ge 0$, define $\gamma C=\{\gamma x\mid x\in C\}$, and for sets $A,B\subseteq \mathbf{R}^n$, define their Minkowski sum by $A+B=\{a+b\mid a\in A,\ b\in B\}$. Show that $C$ is convex if and only if
$$(\alpha+\beta)C=\alpha C+\beta C$$
for all nonnegative scalars $\alpha,\beta\ge 0$.

Exercise 1.3
\emph{Composition of linear-fractional functions.} Let $A \in \mathbf{R}^{m\times n}$, $b \in \mathbf{R}^m$, $c \in \mathbf{R}^n$, $d \in \mathbf{R}$, and let $E \in \mathbf{R}^{p\times m}$, $f \in \mathbf{R}^p$, $g \in \mathbf{R}^m$, $h \in \mathbf{R}$. Suppose $\phi : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}^m$ and $\psi : \mathbf{R}^m \to \mathbf{R}^p$ are the linear-fractional functions

\[
\phi(x) = \frac{Ax + b}{c^T x + d}, \qquad
\psi(y) = \frac{Ey + f}{g^T y + h},
\]

with domains
\[
\mathbf{dom}\,\phi = \{x \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid c^T x + d > 0\},
\qquad
\mathbf{dom}\,\psi = \{y \in \mathbf{R}^m \mid g^T y + h > 0\}.
\]
We associate with $\phi$ and $\psi$ the matrices

\[
\begin{bmatrix}
A & b \\
c^T & d
\end{bmatrix},
\qquad
\begin{bmatrix}
E & f \\
g^T & h
\end{bmatrix},
\]

respectively.

Now consider the composition $\Gamma = \psi \circ \phi$, i.e., $\Gamma(x) = \psi(\phi(x))$, with domain

\[
\mathbf{dom}\,\Gamma = \{x \in \mathbf{dom}\,\phi \mid \phi(x) \in \mathbf{dom}\,\psi\}.
\]

Show that $\Gamma$ is linear-fractional, and that the matrix associated with it is the product

\[
\begin{bmatrix}
E & f \\
g^T & h
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
A & b \\
c^T & d
\end{bmatrix}.
\]

Exercise 1.4
Dual of exponential cone. Let
\[
K_{\exp} = \{(x,y,z) \in \mathbf{R}^3 \mid y > 0,\ ye^{x/y} \le z\}
\]
be the exponential cone in \(\mathbf{R}^3\). Find its dual cone
\[
K_{\exp}^* = \{u \in \mathbf{R}^3 \mid u^T v \ge 0 \text{ for all } v \in K_{\exp}\}.
\]

You may ignore boundary issues. For reference, \(K_{\exp}\) as defined above is not closed; its closure is obtained by adding the points
\[
\{(x,y,z) \in \mathbf{R}^3 \mid x \le 0,\ y = 0,\ z \ge 0\}.
\]
This does not affect the answer, since the dual of a cone is equal to the dual of its closure.

Exercise 1.5
Dual of intersection of cones. Let $C,D\subseteq \mathbf{R}^n$ be closed convex cones. For a cone $K\subseteq \mathbf{R}^n$, its dual cone is defined by
\[
K^* = \{y\in \mathbf{R}^n\mid y^T x \ge 0\ \text{for all } x\in K\}.
\]
In this problem we will show that

\[
(C \cap D)^* = C^* + D^*.
\]

Here, $+$ denotes set addition: $C^* + D^*$ is the set $\{u + v \mid u \in C^*,\ v \in D^*\}$. In other words, the dual of the intersection of two closed convex cones is the sum of the dual cones.

(a) Show that $C \cap D$ and $C^* + D^*$ are convex cones. (In fact, $C \cap D$ and $C^* + D^*$ are closed, but you are not asked to show this.)

Exercise 1.5
Dual of intersection of cones. Let $C,D\subseteq \mathbf{R}^n$ be closed convex cones, and for any cone $K\subseteq \mathbf{R}^n$ define its dual cone by
\[
K^* = \{y\in \mathbf{R}^n\mid y^Tx\ge 0\ \text{for all } x\in K\}.
\]
In this problem we will show that
\[
(C \cap D)^* = C^* + D^*.
\]
Here, $+$ denotes set addition: $C^* + D^*$ is the set $\{u + v \mid u \in C^*,\ v \in D^*\}$. In other words, the dual of the intersection of two closed convex cones is the sum of the dual cones.

(b) Show that
\[
(C \cap D)^* \supseteq C^* + D^*.
\]

Exercise 1.5
Dual of intersection of cones. Let $C$ and $D$ be nonempty closed convex cones in $\mathbf{R}^n$. For a cone $K \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n$, its dual cone is defined by
\[
K^* = \{y \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid y^T x \ge 0 \text{ for all } x \in K\}.
\]
In this problem we will show that

\[
(C \cap D)^* = C^* + D^*.
\]

Here, $+$ denotes set addition: $C^* + D^*$ is the set $\{u + v \mid u \in C^*,\ v \in D^*\}$. In other words, the dual of the intersection of two closed convex cones is the sum of the dual cones.

(c) Now show that $(C \cap D)^* \subseteq C^* + D^*$. You can do this by first proving

\[
(C \cap D)^* \subseteq C^* + D^* \Longleftrightarrow C \cap D \supseteq (C^* + D^*)^* .
\]

You may use the following result:

\[
\text{If } K \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n \text{ is a closed convex cone, then } K^{**} = K.
\]

Next, show that $C \cap D \supseteq (C^* + D^*)^*$ and conclude that $(C \cap D)^* = C^* + D^*$.

Exercise 1.5
Dual of intersection of cones. Let $C$ and $D$ be closed convex cones in $\mathbf{R}^n$. For a cone $K \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n$, its dual cone is defined by
\[
K^* = \{y \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid y^T x \ge 0 \text{ for all } x \in K\}.
\]
In this problem we will show that

\[
(C \cap D)^* = C^* + D^*.
\]

Here, $+$ denotes set addition: $C^* + D^*$ is the set $\{u + v \mid u \in C^*,\ v \in D^*\}$. In other words, the dual of the intersection of two closed convex cones is the sum of the dual cones.

(d) Let $A \in \mathbf{R}^{m \times n}$, and define the polyhedral cone
\[
V = \{x \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid Ax \succeq 0\},
\]
where $Ax \succeq 0$ means componentwise inequality. Show that
\[
V^* = \{A^T v \mid v \in \mathbf{R}^m,\ v \succeq 0\}.
\]

Exercise 1.6
Polar of a set. Let $C$ be an arbitrary subset of $\mathbf{R}^n$. The polar of $C$ is defined by

\[
C^\circ = \{y \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid y^T x \leq 1 \text{ for all } x \in C\}.
\]

(a) Show that $C^\circ$ is convex, even if $C$ itself is not convex.

Exercise 1.6
Polar of a set. Let $C \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n$. The polar of $C$ is defined as

\[
C^\circ = \{y \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid y^T x \leq 1 \text{ for all } x \in C\}.
\]

(b) Suppose that $C$ is a cone in $\mathbf{R}^n$, i.e., $x \in C$ and $\theta \ge 0$ imply $\theta x \in C$. What is the polar $C^\circ$ of $C$?

Exercise 1.6
Polar of a set. Let $\|\cdot\|$ be a norm on $\mathbf{R}^n$, and let its unit ball be
\[
C=\{x\in\mathbf{R}^n\mid \|x\|\le 1\}.
\]
The polar of a set $C\subseteq \mathbf{R}^n$ is defined by
\[
C^\circ = \{y \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid y^T x \leq 1 \text{ for all } x \in C\}.
\]
(c) Determine the polar $C^\circ$ of the unit ball of the norm $\|\cdot\|$.

Exercise 1.6
Polar of a set. For a set $C \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n$, its polar is defined by

\[
C^\circ = \{y \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid y^T x \leq 1 \text{ for all } x \in C\}.
\]

(d) Let $\mathbf{1} \in \mathbf{R}^n$ denote the all-ones vector, and consider the set
\[
C = \{x \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid \mathbf{1}^T x = 1,\, x \succeq 0\}.
\]
What is the polar set $C^\circ$?

Exercise 1.6
Polar of a set. Let $C \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n$. The polar of $C$ is defined by

\[
C^\circ = \{y \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid y^T x \leq 1 \text{ for all } x \in C\}.
\]

(e) Show that if $C$ is a closed convex set and $0 \in C$, then
\[
(C^\circ)^\circ = C.
\]

Exercise 1.7
Dual cones in $\mathbf{R}^2$. For each cone $K\subseteq \mathbf{R}^2$, describe its dual cone, where the dual cone is defined by
\[
K^* = \{y\in \mathbf{R}^2 \mid y^T x \ge 0 \text{ for all } x\in K\}.
\]

(a) $K = \{0\}$.

Exercise 1.7
Dual cones in $\mathbf{R}^2$. For a cone $K \subseteq \mathbf{R}^2$, its dual cone is defined by
\[
K^* = \{y \in \mathbf{R}^2 \mid y^T x \ge 0 \text{ for all } x \in K\}.
\]
Describe the dual cone of the following cone:

(b) $K = \mathbf{R}^2$.

Exercise 1.7
Dual cones in $\mathbf{R}^2$. For the cone $K \subseteq \mathbf{R}^2$, describe its dual cone
\[
K^* = \{y \in \mathbf{R}^2 \mid y^T x \ge 0 \text{ for all } x \in K\}.
\]

(c) $K = \{(x_1, x_2) \in \mathbf{R}^2 \mid |x_1| \leq x_2\}$.

Exercise 1.7
Dual cones in $\mathbf{R}^2$. For the cone $K \subseteq \mathbf{R}^2$ defined by
\[
K = \{(x_1, x_2) \in \mathbf{R}^2 \mid x_1 + x_2 = 0\},
\]
describe its dual cone
\[
K^* = \{y \in \mathbf{R}^2 \mid y^T x \ge 0 \text{ for all } x \in K\}.
\]

(d) $K = \{(x_1, x_2) \in \mathbf{R}^2 \mid x_1 + x_2 = 0\}$.

Exercise 2.1
Maximum of a convex function over a polyhedron. Let $v_1,\ldots,v_k\in\mathbb{R}^n$, and let
\[
P=\operatorname{conv}\{v_1,\ldots,v_k\}
\]
be their convex hull. Let $f:P\to\mathbb{R}$ be a convex function. Show that the maximum of $f$ over $P$ is achieved at one of the points $v_1,\ldots,v_k$, i.e.,

\[
\sup_{x\in P} f(x)=\max_{i=1,\ldots,k} f(v_i).
\]

(A stronger statement is: if $C\subseteq\mathbb{R}^n$ is a closed bounded convex set and $f:C\to\mathbb{R}$ is convex, then the maximum of $f$ over $C$ is achieved at an extreme point of $C$, i.e., a point in $C$ that is not a convex combination of any other points in $C$.) \emph{Hint.} Assume the statement is false, and use Jensen's inequality.

Exercise 2.2
\emph{A general vector composition rule.} Let $h:\mathbf{R}^k\to\mathbf{R}$ be a convex function with $\operatorname{dom} h=\mathbf{R}^k$, and let $g_i:\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}$ for $i=1,\dots,k$. Define

\[
f(x)=h(g_1(x),g_2(x),\ldots,g_k(x)), \qquad x\in\mathbf{R}^n.
\]

Assume that for each $i\in\{1,\dots,k\}$, at least one of the following holds:

- $h$ is nondecreasing in its $i$th argument, and $g_i$ is convex;

- $h$ is nonincreasing in its $i$th argument, and $g_i$ is concave;

- $g_i$ is affine.

Show that $f$ is convex on $\mathbf{R}^n$. (This composition rule subsumes all the ones given in the book, and is the one used in software systems such as CVX. The result also holds in the general case when the monotonicity conditions listed above are imposed on $\tilde h$, the extended-valued extension of $h$.)

Exercise 2.3
\emph{Logarithmic barrier for the second-order cone.} Let
\[
f(x,t)=-\log(t^2-x^Tx),
\]
defined on
\[
\operatorname{dom} f=\{(x,t)\in \mathbf{R}^n\times\mathbf{R}\mid t>\|x\|_2\},
\]
which is the interior of the second-order cone. The function $f$ is convex. (The function $f$ is called the logarithmic barrier function for the second-order cone.) This can be shown in many ways, for example by evaluating the Hessian and demonstrating that it is positive semidefinite. In this exercise you establish convexity of $f$ using a relatively painless method, leveraging some composition rules and known convexity of a few other functions.

(a) Let $u\in \mathbf{R}^n$ and $t\in \mathbf{R}$. Explain why the function
\[
(u,t)\mapsto t-(1/t)u^Tu
\]
is concave on its natural domain
\[
\{(u,t)\in \mathbf{R}^n\times\mathbf{R}\mid t>0\}.
\]
\emph{Hint.} Use convexity of the quadratic-over-linear function.

Exercise 2.3
\emph{Logarithmic barrier for the second-order cone.} Let
\[
f(x,t)=-\log(t^2-x^Tx),
\]
with domain
\[
\operatorname{dom} f=\{(x,t)\in \mathbf{R}^n\times\mathbf{R}\mid t>\|x\|_2\},
\]
which is the interior of the second-order cone. The function $f$ is convex. (The function $f$ is called the logarithmic barrier function for the second-order cone.) This can be shown in many ways, for example by evaluating the Hessian and demonstrating that it is positive semidefinite. In this exercise you establish convexity of $f$ using a relatively painless method, leveraging some composition rules and known convexity of a few other functions.

(b) Using the result from part (a), show that the function
\[
(u,t)\mapsto -\log\!\left(t-\frac{1}{t}u^Tu\right)
\]
is convex on its natural domain
\[
\{(u,t)\in \mathbf{R}^n\times\mathbf{R}_{++}\mid t-(1/t)u^Tu>0\}
=\{(u,t)\in \mathbf{R}^n\times\mathbf{R}\mid t>\|u\|_2\}.
\]

Exercise 2.3
\emph{Logarithmic barrier for the second-order cone.} Let
\[
f(x,t)=-\log(t^2-x^Tx),
\]
with domain
\[
\operatorname{dom} f=\{(x,t)\in \mathbf{R}^n\times\mathbf{R}\mid t>\|x\|_2\}.
\]
Assume, as established in parts (a) and (b), that the functions
\[
(x,t)\mapsto -\log(t-\|x\|_2)
\quad\text{and}\quad
(x,t)\mapsto -\log(t+\|x\|_2)
\]
are convex on this domain. Using the identity
\[
t^2-x^Tx=(t-\|x\|_2)(t+\|x\|_2),
\]
show that $f$ is convex on $\operatorname{dom} f$.

Exercise 2.4
\emph{A quadratic-over-linear composition theorem.} Let $f:\mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}$ be a convex function satisfying $f(x)\geq 0$ for all $x\in \operatorname{dom} f$, and let $g:\mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}$ be a concave function satisfying $g(x)>0$ for all $x\in \operatorname{dom} g$. Show that the function
\[
h(x)=\frac{(f(x))^2}{g(x)},
\]
defined on $\operatorname{dom} h=\operatorname{dom} f\cap \operatorname{dom} g$, is convex.

Exercise 2.5
\emph{A perspective composition rule} [Maréchal]. Let $f:\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}$ be a convex function satisfying $f(0)\leq 0$. Define its perspective by
\[
g(x,t)=t\,f(x/t),\qquad t>0,
\]
so the domain of $g$ is $\{(x,t)\in\mathbf{R}^n\times\mathbf{R}_{++}\}$. 

(a) Show that for each fixed $x\in\mathbf{R}^n$, the function $t\mapsto g(x,t)=t f(x/t)$ is nonincreasing on $(0,\infty)$.

Exercise 2.5
\emph{A perspective composition rule} [Maréchal]. Let $f:\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}$ be a convex function with $f(0)\leq 0$.

(b) Let $g:\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}$ be a concave function on its convex domain, and assume that $g(x)>0$ for every $x\in\operatorname{dom} g$. Define

\[
h(x)=g(x)f\!\left(\frac{x}{g(x)}\right),
\qquad
\operatorname{dom} h=\left\{x\in \operatorname{dom} g\mid \frac{x}{g(x)}\in \operatorname{dom} f\right\}.
\]

Show that $h$ is convex on $\operatorname{dom} h$.

Exercise 2.5
\emph{A perspective composition rule} [Maréchal]. Let $f:\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}$ be a convex function with $f(0)\leq 0$.

(c) As an example, show that the function $h:\mathbf{R}_{++}^n\to\mathbf{R}$ defined by

\[
h(x)=\frac{x^Tx}{\left(\prod_{k=1}^n x_k\right)^{1/n}}, \qquad x=(x_1,\dots,x_n)\in\mathbf{R}_{++}^n,
\]

is convex on its domain $\mathbf{R}_{++}^n$.

Exercise 2.6
Perspective of log determinant. Let $n\in\mathbb{N}$, let $\mathbf{S}_{++}^n$ denote the set of $n\times n$ real symmetric positive definite matrices, and let
\[
f(X,t)=nt\log t-t\log\det X,
\qquad \operatorname{dom} f=\mathbf{S}_{++}^n\times \mathbf{R}_{++}.
\]
Show that $f$ is convex in $(X,t)$. Use this to show that the function $g:\mathbf{S}_{++}^n\to\mathbb{R}$ defined by
\[
g(X)=n(\operatorname{tr}X)\log(\operatorname{tr}X)-(\operatorname{tr}X)(\log\det X)
\]
is convex on $\mathbf{S}_{++}^n$. Equivalently, if $\lambda_1,\dots,\lambda_n>0$ are the eigenvalues of $X$, show that
\[
g(X)=n\left(\sum_{i=1}^n \lambda_i\right)\log\left(\sum_{i=1}^n \lambda_i\right)-\left(\sum_{i=1}^n \lambda_i\right)\log\left(\prod_{i=1}^n \lambda_i\right)
\]
\[
= n\left(\sum_{i=1}^n \lambda_i\right)\left(\log\sum_{i=1}^n \lambda_i-\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n \log\lambda_i\right).
\]

Exercise 2.7
Pre-composition with a linear fractional mapping. Suppose $f:\mathbf{R}^m\to\mathbf{R}$ is convex, and let $A\in\mathbf{R}^{m\times n}$, $b\in\mathbf{R}^m$, $c\in\mathbf{R}^n$, and $d\in\mathbf{R}$. Define $g$ on
\[
\operatorname{dom} g=\{x\in\mathbf{R}^n\mid c^Tx+d>0\}
\]
by
\[
g(x)=(c^Tx+d)\,f\!\left(\frac{Ax+b}{c^Tx+d}\right),
\]
where $\frac{Ax+b}{c^Tx+d}$ denotes division of the vector $Ax+b$ by the positive scalar $c^Tx+d$. Show that $g$ is a convex function on $\operatorname{dom} g$.

Exercise 2.8
Scalar-valued linear fractional functions. Let $a,c\in\mathbf{R}^n$ and $b,d\in\mathbf{R}$, and define $f:\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}$ by
\[
f(x)=\frac{a^T x+b}{c^T x+d},
\]
with domain
\[
\operatorname{dom} f=\{x\in\mathbf{R}^n\mid c^T x+d>0\}.
\]
A function of this form is called linear fractional. Determine for which choices of $a,b,c,d$ the function $f$ is convex on its domain, and for which choices of $a,b,c,d$ the function $f$ is quasiconvex on its domain.

Exercise 2.9
Let $A\in\mathbb{R}^{m\times n}$ and $b\in\mathbb{R}^m$, and define $f:\{x\in\mathbb{R}^n\mid \|x\|_2<1\}\to\mathbb{R}$ by

\[
f(x)=\frac{\|Ax-b\|_2^2}{1-x^Tx}.
\]

Show that $f$ is convex on $\{x\in\mathbb{R}^n\mid \|x\|_2 < 1\}$.

Exercise 2.10
Weighted geometric mean. Let
\[
f(x)=\prod_{k=1}^n x_k^{\alpha_k}, \qquad x=(x_1,\dots,x_n)\in \mathbf{R}_{++}^n,
\]
where the weights satisfy \(\alpha_k\ge 0\) for \(k=1,\dots,n\) and \(\sum_{k=1}^n \alpha_k\le 1\). The geometric mean \(\left(\prod_{k=1}^n x_k\right)^{1/n}\) is known to be concave on \(\mathbf{R}_{++}^n\). Extend that proof to show that the weighted geometric mean \(f\) is concave on \(\mathbf{R}_{++}^n\).

Exercise 2.11
Let $f:\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}$ be a convex function. Define $g$ on $\mathbf{R}^n\times \mathbf{R}_{++}$, where $\mathbf{R}_{++}=(0,\infty)$, by

\[
g(x,t)=f(x/t), \qquad (x,t)\in \mathbf{R}^n\times \mathbf{R}_{++}.
\]

Show that $g$ is quasiconvex on its domain.

Exercise 2.12
Continued fraction function. Consider the function $f:D\to\mathbb{R}$ defined by

\[
f(x)=\frac{1}{x_1-\frac{1}{x_2-\frac{1}{x_3-\frac{1}{x_4}}}},
\]

where $x=(x_1,x_2,x_3,x_4)\in\mathbb{R}^4$ and the domain $D$ consists of all points for which every denominator appearing in the continued fraction is positive, i.e.,
\[
x_4>0,\qquad x_3-\frac{1}{x_4}>0,\qquad x_2-\frac{1}{x_3-\frac{1}{x_4}}>0,\qquad x_1-\frac{1}{x_2-\frac{1}{x_3-\frac{1}{x_4}}}>0.
\]
Show that $f$ is convex on $D$ and decreasing in each variable on $D$. (There is nothing special about $n=4$ here; the same holds for any number of variables.)

Exercise 2.13
Circularly symmetric Huber function. Let $n\ge 1$ and $x\in \mathbf{R}^n$. The scalar Huber function is defined for $t\in \mathbf{R}$ by

\[
f_{\mathrm{hub}}(t)=
\begin{cases}
(1/2)t^2 & |t|\leq 1,\\
|t|-1/2 & |t|>1.
\end{cases}
\]

This convex function arises in several applications, including robust estimation. One generalization to $\mathbf{R}^n$ is $f_{\mathrm{hub}}(x_1)+\cdots+f_{\mathrm{hub}}(x_n)$, but this function is not circularly symmetric, i.e., it is not invariant under the transformation $x\mapsto Qx$ for orthogonal matrices $Q\in \mathbf{R}^{n\times n}$. A circularly symmetric generalization to $\mathbf{R}^n$ is

\[
f_{\mathrm{cshub}}(x)=f_{\mathrm{hub}}(\|x\|_2)=
\begin{cases}
(1/2)\|x\|_2^2 & \|x\|_2\leq 1,\\
\|x\|_2-1/2 & \|x\|_2>1.
\end{cases}
\]

(The subscript stands for `circularly symmetric Huber function'.) Show that $f_{\mathrm{cshub}}:\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}$ is convex. Then find its Fenchel conjugate
\[
f_{\mathrm{cshub}}^*(y)=\sup_{x\in\mathbf{R}^n}\bigl(y^Tx-f_{\mathrm{cshub}}(x)\bigr), \qquad y\in\mathbf{R}^n.
\]

Exercise 2.14
Reverse Jensen inequality. Suppose $f:\mathbb{R}^m\to\mathbb{R}\cup\{+\infty\}$ is convex, and let $x_1,\ldots,x_n\in\operatorname{dom} f$. Assume the scalars $\lambda_1,\ldots,\lambda_n$ satisfy $\lambda_1>0$, $\lambda_i\leq 0$ for $i=2,\ldots,n$, and $\lambda_1+\cdots+\lambda_n=1$. Show that

\[
f(\lambda_1x_1+\cdots+\lambda_nx_n)\geq \lambda_1f(x_1)+\cdots+\lambda_nf(x_n).
\]

Hints. Draw a picture for the $n=2$ case first. For the general case, express $x_1$ as a convex combination of $\lambda_1x_1+\cdots+\lambda_nx_n$ and $x_2,\ldots,x_n$, and use Jensen's inequality.

Exercise 2.15
Monotone extension of a convex function. Suppose $f:\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}$ is convex. For vectors in $\mathbf{R}^n$, inequalities are understood componentwise: $x\geq y$ means $x_i\geq y_i$ for all $i$, and $z\geq 0$ means $z\in\mathbf{R}^n$ with $z_i\geq 0$ for all $i$. Recall that a function $h:\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}$ is monotone nondecreasing if $h(x)\geq h(y)$ whenever $x\geq y$. The monotone extension of $f$ is defined as

\[
g(x)=\inf_{z\geq 0} f(x+z), \qquad x\in\mathbf{R}^n.
\]

Assume that $g(x)>-\infty$ for all $x\in\mathbf{R}^n$. Show that $g$ is convex and monotone nondecreasing, and satisfies $g(x)\leq f(x)$ for all $x\in\mathbf{R}^n$. Show that if $h:\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}$ is any other convex function that is monotone nondecreasing and satisfies $h(x)\leq f(x)$ for all $x\in\mathbf{R}^n$, then $h(x)\leq g(x)$ for all $x\in\mathbf{R}^n$. Thus, $g$ is the maximum convex monotone underestimator of $f$.

Remark. For simple functions (say, on $\mathbf{R}$) it is easy to work out what $g$ is, given $f$. On $\mathbf{R}^n$, it can be very difficult to work out an explicit expression for $g$. However, systems such as CVX can immediately handle functions such as $g$, defined by partial minimization.

Exercise 2.16
\emph{Circularly symmetric convex functions.} Let $f:\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}$ be a convex function that is invariant under rotations, i.e., $f(Qx)=f(x)$ for every orthogonal matrix $Q\in\mathbf{R}^{n\times n}$ (equivalently, $f(x)$ depends only on $\|x\|_2$). Show that there exists a function $\phi:[0,\infty)\to\mathbf{R}$ such that
\[
f(x)=\phi(\|x\|_2),\qquad x\in\mathbf{R}^n,
\]

Exercise 2.17
\emph{Infimal convolution.} Let $f_1,\ldots,f_m:\mathbf{R}^n\to \mathbf{R}\cup\{+\infty\}$ be convex functions. Their infimal convolution, denoted $g=f_1 \diamond \cdots \diamond f_m$ (several other notations are also used), is defined for each $x\in\mathbf{R}^n$ by

\[
g(x)=\inf\{f_1(x_1)+\cdots+f_m(x_m)\mid x_1,\ldots,x_m\in\mathbf{R}^n,\ x_1+\cdots+x_m=x\},
\]

with effective domain $\operatorname{dom} g=\{x\in\mathbf{R}^n\mid g(x)<+\infty\}$. In one simple interpretation, $f_i(x_i)$ is the cost for the $i$th firm to produce a mix of products given by $x_i$; $g(x)$ is then the optimal cost obtained if the firms can freely exchange products to produce, all together, the mix given by $x$. (The name `convolution' presumably comes from the observation that if we replace the sum above with the product, and the infimum above with integration, then we obtain the normal convolution.)

\]
Let $S_C$ denote the support function of $C$,
\[
S_C(y)=\sup_{z\in C} y^T z,
\]
and let $S_C^*$ be its convex conjugate,
\[
S_C^*(x)=\sup_{y\in\mathbb{R}^n}(x^Ty-S_C(y)).
\]
Recall that $x\in\mathbb{R}^n$ is majorized by $a$ if, after sorting both vectors in nonincreasing order, say $x_{[1]}\ge\cdots\ge x_{[n]}$ and $a_{[1]}\ge\cdots\ge a_{[n]}$, we have
\[
\sum_{i=1}^k x_{[i]}\le \sum_{i=1}^k a_{[i]},\qquad k=1,\ldots,n-1,
\]
and
\[
\sum_{i=1}^n x_i=\sum_{i=1}^n a_i.
\]

\[
S_C^*(x)=
\begin{cases}
0 & \text{if } x \text{ is majorized by } a,\\
+\infty & \text{otherwise.}
\end{cases}
\]

Exercise 3.10
\emph{Linear programming with random cost vector.} Consider the optimization problem with decision variable $x \in \mathbf{R}^n$,

\]

where $A \in \mathbf{R}^{m \times n}$ and $b \in \mathbf{R}^m$ are deterministic, and the feasible set $\{x \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid Ax \preceq b\}$ is assumed nonempty. Here the cost vector $c \in \mathbf{R}^n$ is random, normally distributed with mean $\mathbf{E}\,c = c_0 \in \mathbf{R}^n$ and covariance matrix $\mathbf{E}(c-c_0)(c-c_0)^T = \Sigma \in \mathbf{S}_+^n$. Thus, for a given feasible $x$, the cost $c^T x$ is a scalar Gaussian random variable.

We can attach several different meanings to the goal `minimize $c^T x$'; we explore some of these below.

(b) In general there is a tradeoff between small expected cost and small cost variance. One way to take variance into account is to minimize, over all feasible $x$, the linear combination

\[
\mathbf{E}\,c^T x + \gamma \, \operatorname{var}(c^T x)
\tag{3}
\]

of the expected value $\mathbf{E}\, c^T x$ and the variance $\operatorname{var}(c^T x) = \mathbf{E}(c^T x)^2 - (\mathbf{E}\, c^T x)^2$, where $\gamma \geq 0$ is a given parameter. This is called the `risk-sensitive cost', and $\gamma$ is called the \emph{risk-aversion parameter}, since it sets the relative values of cost variance and expected value. (For $\gamma > 0$, we are willing to trade off an increase in expected cost for a decrease in cost variance.) How would you minimize the risk-sensitive cost? Is this problem a convex optimization problem? Be as specific as you can.

Exercise 3.15
\emph{Transforming a standard form convex problem to conic form.} In this problem, we show that any convex problem can be cast in conic form, provided some technical conditions hold. Consider the standard-form convex optimization problem

\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{minimize}\quad & c^T x \\
\text{subject to}\quad & f_i(x)\leq 0,\quad i=1,\ldots,m, \\
& Ax=b,
\end{aligned}
\]

where $x\in \mathbf{R}^n$ is the optimization variable, $c\in \mathbf{R}^n$, $A\in \mathbf{R}^{p\times n}$, $b\in \mathbf{R}^p$, and each function $f_i:\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}$ is convex. For simplicity, assume that $\operatorname{dom} f_i=\mathbf{R}^n$ for each $i=1,\ldots,m$.

Now introduce a new scalar variable $t\in\mathbf{R}$ and consider the equivalent problem

\[
\begin{array}{ll}
\mbox{minimize} & c^T x \\
\mbox{subject to} & t f_i(x/t) \leq 0, \quad i=1,\ldots,m, \\
& Ax=b, \quad t=1,
\end{array}
\]

where the expressions $f_i(x/t)$ are interpreted only for $t>0$.

Define the set

\[
K=\operatorname{cl}\{(x,t)\in \mathbf{R}^{n+1}\mid t>0,\ t f_i(x/t)\leq 0,\ i=1,\ldots,m\}.
\]

Then the original problem can be written as

\[
\begin{array}{ll}
\mbox{minimize} & c^T x \\
\mbox{subject to} & (x,t)\in K, \\
& Ax=b, \quad t=1.
\end{array}
\]

This is a conic problem when $K$ is a proper cone. Relate properties of the original problem to $K$.

(a) Show that $K$ is a convex cone. (It is closed by definition, since we take the closure.)

Exercise 3.16
\emph{Exploring nearly optimal points.} Consider the optimization problem

\[
\begin{array}{ll}
\mbox{minimize} & f_0(x) \\
\mbox{subject to} & f_i(x)\leq 0, \quad i=1,\ldots,m, \\

\end{array}
\]
with variable $x \in \mathbf{R}^n$, and suppose the problem is feasible. Let $p^\star$ denote its optimal value and let $x^\star$ be an optimal point. To explore the set of nearly optimal points, for a given $\epsilon>0$ and vector $c\in\mathbf{R}^n$, one considers the auxiliary problem

\[
\begin{array}{ll}
\mbox{minimize} & c^T x \\
\mbox{subject to} & f_i(x)\leq 0, \quad i=1,\ldots,m, \\
& h_i(x)=0, \quad i=1,\ldots,p, \\
& f_0(x)\leq p^\star+\epsilon.
\end{array}
\]

Any feasible point of this auxiliary problem is $\epsilon$-suboptimal for the original problem. By solving it for several choices of $c$, one can assess whether the original problem has a strong minimum (all nearly optimal points are close) or a weak minimum (there are quite different nearly optimal points).

\sin\theta_1
\end{bmatrix}u_1(k)
+

\sin\theta_2
\end{bmatrix}u_2(k)

\end{bmatrix},

where $m>0$ is the vehicle mass, $\alpha\in(0,1)$ is the drag parameter, $\theta_1,\theta_2\in\mathbf{R}$ are the fixed thrust directions, and $g=10$ is the gravitational acceleration. The dynamics are

The total fuel use is

Exercise 3.16
\emph{Exploring nearly optimal points.} Assume the optimization problem below is feasible and attains its optimal value. An optimization algorithm will then find an optimal point for the problem. It is often useful to explore the set of nearly optimal points. When a problem has a `strong minimum', the set of nearly optimal points is small; all such points are close to the original optimal point found. At the other extreme, a problem can have a `soft minimum', which means that there are many points, some quite far from the original optimal point found, that are feasible and have nearly optimal objective value. In this problem you will use a typical method to explore the set of nearly optimal points.

We start by finding the optimal value $p^\star$ of the given problem

\[
\begin{array}{ll}
\mbox{minimize} & f_0(x) \\
\mbox{subject to} & f_i(x)\leq 0, \quad i=1,\ldots,m \\
& h_i(x)=0, \quad i=1,\ldots,p,
\end{array}
\]

as well as an optimal point $x^\star \in \mathbf{R}^n$. We then pick a small positive number $\epsilon$, and a vector $c \in \mathbf{R}^n$, and solve the problem

\[
\begin{array}{ll}
\mbox{minimize} & c^T x \\
\mbox{subject to} & f_i(x)\leq 0, \quad i=1,\ldots,m \\
& h_i(x)=0, \quad i=1,\ldots,p \\
& f_0(x)\leq p^\star + \epsilon.
\end{array}
\]

Note that any feasible point for this problem is $\epsilon$-suboptimal for the original problem. Solving this problem multiple times, with different $c$'s, will generate (perhaps different) $\epsilon$-suboptimal points. If the problem has a strong minimum, these points will all be close to each other; if the problem has a weak minimum, they can be quite different.

There are different strategies for choosing $c$ in these experiments. The simplest is to choose the $c$'s randomly; another method is to choose $c$ to have the form $\pm e_i$, for $i = 1, \ldots, n$. (This method gives the `range' of each component of $x$, over the $\epsilon$-suboptimal set.)

You will carry out this method for the following problem, to determine whether it has a strong minimum or a weak minimum. You can generate the vectors $c$ randomly, with enough samples for you to come to your conclusion. You can pick $\epsilon = 0.01p^\star$, which means that we are considering the set of $1\%$-suboptimal points.

The problem is a minimum-fuel optimal control problem for a vehicle moving in $\mathbf{R}^2$. Let $p(k) \in \mathbf{R}^2$ denote the position and $v(k) \in \mathbf{R}^2$ the velocity at time $kh$, for $k = 1, \ldots, K$, where $h > 0$ is the sampling period. These are related by the dynamics

\[
p(k+1) = p(k) + hv(k), \qquad v(k+1) = (1-\alpha)v(k) + (h/m)f(k), \qquad k = 1, \ldots, K-1,
\]

where $f(k) \in \mathbf{R}^2$ is the total applied force at time $kh$, $m > 0$ is the vehicle mass, and $\alpha \in (0,1)$ models drag on the vehicle; in the absence of any other force, the vehicle velocity decreases by the factor $1-\alpha$ in each discretized time interval. (These formulas are approximations of more accurate formulas that involve matrix exponentials.)

The force comes from two thrusters and gravity:

\[
f(k) =
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos \theta_1 \\
\sin \theta_1
\end{bmatrix} u_1(k)
+
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos \theta_2 \\
\sin \theta_2
\end{bmatrix} u_2(k)
+
\begin{bmatrix}
0 \\
-mg
\end{bmatrix},
\qquad k = 1, \ldots, K-1,
\]

where $u_1(k),u_2(k) \in \mathbf{R}$ are the nonnegative thruster force magnitudes, i.e., $u_1(k) \ge 0$ and $u_2(k) \ge 0$, $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$ are the fixed directions of the thrust forces, and $g = 10$ is the constant acceleration due to gravity.

The total fuel use is

\[
F = \sum_{k=1}^{K-1} (u_1(k) + u_2(k)).
\]

The optimization problem is to minimize $F$ over the variables $p(1),\ldots,p(K)$, $v(1),\ldots,v(K)$, and $u_1(1),\ldots,u_1(K-1)$, $u_2(1),\ldots,u_2(K-1)$, subject to the initial conditions $p(1) = 0$, $v(1) = 0$, the dynamics above, the nonnegativity constraints on the thruster magnitudes, and the way-point constraints

\[
p(k_i) = w_i, \qquad i = 1, \ldots, M,
\]

where each $k_i \in \{1,\ldots,K\}$ and $w_i \in \mathbf{R}^2$ are given. In addition, the vehicle must remain in the square operating region

\[
\|p(k)\|_\infty \leq P^{\max}, \qquad k = 1, \ldots, K,
\]

where $P^{\max} > 0$ is given.

Both parts of this problem concern the specific problem instance with data given in \texttt{thrusters\_data.*}.

(b) Generate several $1\%$-suboptimal trajectories using the general method described above, and plot the associated trajectories in $\mathbf{R}^2$. Would you say this problem has a strong minimum, or a weak minimum?

Exercise 3.18
\emph{Heuristic suboptimal solution for Boolean LP.} This exercise builds on 4.15 and 5.13 in \emph{Convex Optimization}, which involve the Boolean LP

\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{minimize}\quad & c^T x \\
\text{subject to}\quad & Ax \preceq b \\
& x_i \in \{0,1\}, \quad i=1,\ldots,n,
\end{aligned}
\]

where $x\in\mathbf{R}^n$, $A\in\mathbf{R}^{m\times n}$, $b\in\mathbf{R}^m$, and $c\in\mathbf{R}^n$, and let the optimal value be $p^\star$. Let $x^{\mathrm{rlx}}$ be a solution of the LP relaxation

\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{minimize}\quad & c^T x \\
\text{subject to}\quad & Ax \preceq b \\
& 0 \preceq x \preceq 1,
\end{aligned}
\]

assuming the relaxation is feasible, so $L=c^T x^{\mathrm{rlx}}$ is a lower bound on $p^\star$. The relaxed solution $x^{\mathrm{rlx}}$ can also be used to guess a Boolean point $\hat{x}$, by rounding its entries, based on a threshold $t\in[0,1]$:

\[
\hat{x}_i=
\begin{cases}
1 & x_i^{\mathrm{rlx}} \geq t\\
0 & \text{otherwise},
\end{cases}
\]

for $i=1,\ldots,n$. Evidently $\hat{x}$ is Boolean (\emph{i.e.}, has entries in $\{0,1\}$). If it is feasible for the Boolean LP, \emph{i.e.}, if $A\hat{x}\preceq b$, then it can be considered a guess at a good, if not optimal, point for the Boolean LP. Its objective value, $U=c^T\hat{x}$, is an upper bound on $p^\star$. If $U$ and $L$ are close, then $\hat{x}$ is nearly optimal; specifically, $\hat{x}$ cannot be more than $(U-L)$-suboptimal for the Boolean LP.

This rounding need not work; indeed, it can happen that for all threshold values, $\hat{x}$ is infeasible. But for some problem instances, it can work well.

Of course, there are many variations on this simple scheme for (possibly) constructing a feasible, good point from $x^{\mathrm{rlx}}$.

Finally, we get to the problem. Generate a Boolean LP instance of the above form with positive resource data, i.e., choose dimensions $m,n$ and data satisfying $A_{ij}>0$ and $b_i>0$ for all relevant $i,j$, in such a way that the LP relaxation is feasible.

You can think of $x_i$ as a job we either accept or decline, and $-c_i$ as the (positive) revenue we generate if we accept job $i$. We can think of $Ax \preceq b$ as a set of limits on $m$ resources. $A_{ij}$, which is positive, is the amount of resource $i$ consumed if we accept job $j$; $b_i$, which is positive, is the amount of resource $i$ available.

Find a solution of the relaxed LP and examine its entries. Note the associated lower bound $L$. Carry out threshold rounding for (say) 100 values of $t$, uniformly spaced over $[0,1]$. For each value of $t$, note the objective value $c^T \hat{x}$ and the maximum constraint violation $\max_i (A\hat{x}-b)_i$. Plot the objective value and the maximum violation versus $t$. Be sure to indicate on the plot the values of $t$ for which $\hat{x}$ is feasible, and those for which it is not.

Find a value of $t$ for which $\hat{x}$ is feasible, and gives minimum objective value, and note the associated upper bound $U$. Give the gap $U-L$ between the upper bound on $p^\star$ and the lower bound on $p^\star$.

In Matlab, if you define vectors obj and maxviol, you can find the upper bound as `U=min(obj(find(maxviol<=0)));`

Jensen's inequality for posynomials. Let $f:\mathbf{R}_{++}^n\to \mathbf{R}_{++}$ be a posynomial function, i.e.,

where $m\in\mathbf{N}$, $c_k>0$, and $a_{ik}\in\mathbf{R}$ for all $i,k$. Suppose $x,y\in \mathbf{R}_{++}^n$ and $\theta\in[0,1]$. Define $z\in \mathbf{R}_{++}^n$ by

Exercise 3.27
\emph{Affine policy.} Let $u \in \mathbf{R}^p$ be a random variable uniformly distributed on $U=[-1,1]^p$. Consider the family of linear programs

\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{minimize}\quad & c^T x \\
\text{subject to}\quad & Ax \preceq b(u),
\end{aligned}
\]

where $x \in \mathbf{R}^n$, $A \in \mathbf{R}^{m \times n}$, $c \in \mathbf{R}^n$, and
\[
b(u)=b_0+Bu \in \mathbf{R}^m,
\]
with $b_0 \in \mathbf{R}^m$ and $B \in \mathbf{R}^{m\times p}$. You can think of $u_i$ as representing a deviation of the $i$th parameter from its nominal value. The parameters might represent (deviations in) levels of resources available, or other varying limits.

The problem is to be solved many times; each time, the value of $u$ (i.e., a sample) is given, and then the decision variable $x$ is chosen. The mapping from $u$ into the decision variable $x(u)$ is called the \emph{policy}, since it gives the decision variable value for each value of $u$. When enough time and computing hardware is available, we can simply solve the LP for each new value of $u$; this is an optimal policy, which we denote $x^\star(u)$.

In some applications, however, the decision $x(u)$ must be made very quickly, so solving the LP is not an option. Instead we seek a suboptimal policy that is affine:
\[
x^{\mathrm{aff}}(u)=x_0+Ku,
\]
where $x_0 \in \mathbf{R}^n$ is called the nominal decision and $K \in \mathbf{R}^{n\times p}$ is called the feedback gain matrix. (Roughly speaking, $x_0$ is our guess of $x$ before the value of $u$ has been revealed; $Ku$ is our modification of this guess, once we know $u$.) We determine the policy (i.e., suitable values for $x_0$ and $K$) ahead of time; we can then evaluate the policy (that is, find $x^{\mathrm{aff}}(u)$ given $u$) very quickly, by matrix multiplication and addition.

We will choose $x_0$ and $K$ in order to minimize the expected value of the objective, while insisting that feasibility be maintained for every possible realization of $u \in U$:

\[
\begin{array}{ll}
\mbox{minimize} & \mathbf{E}\, c^T x^{\mathrm{aff}}(u) \\
\mbox{subject to} & Ax^{\mathrm{aff}}(u) \preceq b(u) \quad \forall u \in U.
\end{array}
\]

The optimization variables are $x_0$ and $K$. The expectation in the objective is taken with respect to the uniform distribution of $u$ on $U$, and the constraint is required to hold for all $u \in U$.

(a) Explain how to find optimal values of $x_0$ and $K$ by solving a standard explicit convex optimization problem, i.e., one that does not involve either an expectation or infinitely many constraints. Your formulation should have a number of variables and constraints that does not grow exponentially with the problem dimensions $n$, $p$, or $m$.

Exercise 3.31
Stochastic optimization via Monte Carlo sampling. Let $(\Omega,\mathcal{F},\mathbb{P})$ be a probability space, let $\omega$ be an $\Omega$-valued random variable, and let $f:\mathbf{R}^n\times\Omega\to\mathbf{R}$ be such that for each fixed $\omega\in\Omega$, the function $x\mapsto f(x,\omega)$ is convex. Assume that for every $x\in\mathbf{R}^n$, the expectation $F(x)=\mathbf{E}\,f(x,\omega)$ is well defined and finite. Consider the unconstrained stochastic optimization problem
\[
\min_{x\in\mathbf{R}^n} F(x),\qquad F(x)=\mathbf{E}\,f(x,\omega),
\]
and let
\[
p^\star=\inf_{x\in\mathbf{R}^n} F(x),
\]
which is assumed finite.

In a few very simple cases we can work out what $F$ is analytically, but in general this is not possible. Moreover in many applications, we do not know the distribution of $\omega$; we only have access to an oracle that can generate independent samples from the distribution.

A standard method for approximately solving the stochastic optimization problem is based on Monte Carlo sampling. We first generate $N$ independent samples, $\omega_1,\ldots,\omega_N$, each with the same distribution as $\omega$, and form the empirical expectation
\[
\hat{F}(x)=\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^N f(x,\omega_i).
\]
This is a random function, since it depends on the particular samples drawn. For each $x$, we have $\mathbf{E}\,\hat{F}(x)=F(x)$, and also $\mathbf{E}(\hat{F}(x)-F(x))^2\propto 1/N$. Roughly speaking, for $N$ large enough, $\hat{F}(x)\approx F(x)$.

To (approximately) minimize $F$, we instead minimize $\hat{F}(x)$. Let
\[
\hat{p}^\star=\inf_{x\in\mathbf{R}^n}\hat{F}(x).
\]
(If a minimizer exists, denote one by $\hat{x}^\star$ so that $\hat{p}^\star=\hat{F}(\hat{x}^\star)$.) Then $\hat{p}^\star$ is a random variable. The hope is that for $N$ large enough, we have $\hat{p}^\star\approx p^\star$. (In practice, stochastic optimization via Monte Carlo sampling works very well, even when $N$ is not that big.)

One way to check the result of Monte Carlo sampling is to carry it out multiple times. We repeatedly generate different batches of samples, and for each batch, we find $\hat{x}^\star$ and $\hat{p}^\star$. If the values of $\hat{p}^\star$ are near each other, it's reasonable to believe that we have (approximately) minimized $F$. If they are not, it means our value of $N$ is too small.

Show that
\[
\mathbf{E}\,\hat{p}^\star\le p^\star.
\]

Hint. Show that for any function $G:\mathbf{R}^n\times\Omega\to\mathbf{R}$ for which the expectations below are well defined, and any random variable $\omega$ on $\Omega$, we have
\[
\inf_x \mathbf{E}\,G(x,\omega)\ge \mathbf{E}\inf_x G(x,\omega).
\]

\begin{array}{ll}
\mbox{minimize} & \sum_{k=1}^n x_k \log(x_k/y_k) \\
\mbox{subject to} & Ax = b, \\
& \mathbf{1}^T x = 1,
\end{array}
\]
where the optimization variable is $x\in\mathbf{R}^n$, the objective is understood with domain $x\in\mathbf{R}_{++}^n$, and the given data are $y\in\mathbf{R}_{++}^n$, $A\in\mathbf{R}^{m\times n}$, and $b\in\mathbf{R}^m$. Assume the problem is feasible.

Derive the Lagrange dual problem and simplify it to the form
\[
\mbox{maximize}_{z\in\mathbf{R}^m} \quad b^T z - \log \sum_{k=1}^n y_k e^{a_k^T z},
\]
where $a_k$ denotes the $k$th column of $A$.

Exercise 4.4
\emph{Source localization from range measurements} [Beck, Stoica, and Li]. Let $x \in \mathbf{R}^n$ denote the unknown position of a signal source, where $n \in \{2,3\}$. The source is observed by $m$ sensors at known positions $y_1,\ldots,y_m \in \mathbf{R}^n$. From the received signal strengths, noisy estimates $d_k \in \mathbf{R}_+$ of the distances $\|x-y_k\|_2$ are obtained. We wish to estimate the source position $x$ from the measured distances $d_k$.

Consider the least-squares problem in which the error between the squares of the actual and observed distances is minimized:

\[
\mbox{minimize} \quad f_0(x) = \sum_{k=1}^m \left( \|x-y_k\|_2^2 - d_k^2 \right)^2,
\qquad x \in \mathbf{R}^n.
\]

Introducing an additional scalar variable $t \in \mathbf{R}$ with $t = x^T x$, this problem can be written equivalently as

\[
\begin{array}{ll}
\mbox{minimize} & \sum_{k=1}^m \left( t - 2y_k^T x + \|y_k\|_2^2 - d_k^2 \right)^2 \\
\mbox{subject to} & x^T x - t = 0,
\end{array}
\tag{6}
\]

with variables $x \in \mathbf{R}^n$ and $t \in \mathbf{R}$. Although this problem is not convex, it is known that strong duality holds for it. (It is a variation on the problem discussed on page 229 and in exercise 5.29 of \emph{Convex Optimization}.)

Solve problem (6) for the instance with $m=5$ and $n=2$, where

\[
y_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 1.8 \\ 2.5 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad
y_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 2.0 \\ 1.7 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad
y_3 = \begin{bmatrix} 1.5 \\ 1.5 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad
y_4 = \begin{bmatrix} 1.5 \\ 2.0 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad
y_5 = \begin{bmatrix} 2.5 \\ 1.5 \end{bmatrix},
\]

and

\[
d = (2.00,\, 1.24,\, 0.59,\, 1.31,\, 1.44).
\]

To solve the problem, you may use the fact that, if the optimal Lagrange multiplier $\nu^\star \in \mathbf{R}$ associated with the equality constraint in (6) is known, then the corresponding optimal primal solution $x^\star$ can be obtained directly from the KKT conditions. You can use either of the following two methods to determine $\nu^\star$:

\begin{itemize}
\item Derive the dual problem, express it as an SDP, and solve it using CVX.
\item Reduce the KKT conditions to a nonlinear equation in $\nu$, and select the correct solution (similarly as in exercise 5.29 of \emph{Convex Optimization}).
\end{itemize}

Projection on the $\ell_1$ ball.

Let $a \in \mathbf{R}^n$ be given. Consider the problem of projecting $a$ onto the unit ball in the $\ell_1$-norm, i.e., the optimization problem in the variable $x \in \mathbf{R}^n$:

$$
\begin{array}{ll}
\mbox{minimize} & (1/2)\|x-a\|_2^2 \\
\mbox{subject to} & \|x\|_1 \leq 1.
\end{array}
$$

Derive the dual problem and describe an efficient method for solving it. Explain how to obtain the optimal primal solution $x$ from a solution of the dual problem.

Exercise 4.6
A nonconvex problem with strong duality. Consider the optimization problem

\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{minimize}\quad & f(x)=x^T A x + 2b^T x \\
\text{subject to}\quad & x^T x \leq 1
\end{aligned}
\tag{7}
\]

with variable $x \in \mathbf{R}^n$ and data $A \in \mathbf{S}^n$, $b \in \mathbf{R}^n$. We do not assume that $A$ is positive semidefinite, so the problem is not necessarily convex. Suppose $x \in \mathbf{R}^n$ and $\lambda \in \mathbf{R}$ satisfy

\[
\|x\|_2 \leq 1, \qquad \lambda \geq 0, \qquad A+\lambda I \succeq 0, \qquad (A+\lambda I)x=-b, \qquad \lambda(1-\|x\|_2^2)=0.
\tag{8}
\]

These are the KKT conditions for (7), together with the additional condition $A+\lambda I \succeq 0$. Let the Lagrangian be
\[
L(\tilde{x},\lambda)=\tilde{x}^T A\tilde{x}+2b^T\tilde{x}+\lambda(\tilde{x}^T\tilde{x}-1),
\]
and let the dual function be
\[
g(\lambda)=\inf_{\tilde{x}\in\mathbf{R}^n} L(\tilde{x},\lambda).
\]

(a) Show that if $x$ and $\lambda$ satisfy (8), then
\[
f(x)=\inf_{\tilde{x}\in\mathbf{R}^n} L(\tilde{x},\lambda)=g(\lambda).
\]
Therefore strong duality holds, and $x$ is a globally optimal solution of (7).

Exercise 4.6
A nonconvex problem with strong duality. Consider the optimization problem

\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{minimize}\quad & f(x)=x^T A x + 2b^T x \\
\text{subject to}\quad & x^T x \leq 1
\end{aligned}
\tag{7}
\]

with variable $x \in \mathbf{R}^n$, where $A \in \mathbf{S}^n$ is a real symmetric matrix and $b \in \mathbf{R}^n$. We do not assume that $A$ is positive semidefinite, so the problem is not necessarily convex. In this exercise we show that $x$ is a global optimal solution of (7) if and only if there exists a scalar $\lambda \in \mathbf{R}$ such that

\[
\|x\|_2 \leq 1, \qquad \lambda \geq 0, \qquad A+\lambda I \succeq 0, \qquad (A+\lambda I)x=-b, \qquad \lambda(1-\|x\|_2^2)=0.
\tag{8}
\]

The conditions in (8) are the KKT conditions for (7), together with the additional requirement $A+\lambda I \succeq 0$.

(b) Next show that the conditions (8) are also necessary. Assume that $x$ is a globally optimal solution of (7). Distinguish the following two cases.

(i) $\|x\|_2<1$. Show that (8) holds with $\lambda=0$.

(ii) $\|x\|_2=1$. First prove that $(A+\lambda I)x=-b$ for some $\lambda \geq 0$. (In other words, the negative gradient $-(Ax+b)$ of the objective function is normal to the unit sphere at $x$ and points away from the origin.) You can show this by contradiction: if this condition does not hold, then there exists a direction $v \in \mathbf{R}^n$ such that $v^T x<0$ and $v^T(Ax+b)<0$. A nonconvex problem with strong duality. Consider the problem

\[

\tag{7}

with variable $x \in \mathbf{R}^n$, and data $A \in \mathbf{S}^n$, $b \in \mathbf{R}^n$. We do not assume that $A$ is positive semidefinite, so the problem is not necessarily convex. \[

\]
be an eigenvalue decomposition of $A$, where $q_1,\dots,q_n$ are orthonormal eigenvectors and the eigenvalues are ordered as
\[

Exercise 4.7
\emph{Connection between perturbed optimal cost and Lagrange dual functions.} Consider the convex optimization problem

\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{minimize}\quad & f_0(x) \\
\text{subject to}\quad & f_i(x) \leq 0,\quad i=1,\ldots,m,
\end{aligned}
\]

where $x\in\mathbb{R}^n$, the functions $f_0,f_1,\ldots,f_m:\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}\cup\{\infty\}$ are convex, and $\mathcal D=\bigcap_{i=0}^m \operatorname{dom} f_i$ is the common effective domain. We consider only inequality constraints, for simplicity. Define the perturbed optimal value function

\[
p^\star(u)=\inf\{f_0(x)\mid x\in\mathcal{D},\ f_i(x)\leq u_i,\ i=1,\ldots,m\},
\qquad u\in\mathbb{R}^m,
\]

and the Lagrange dual function

\[
g(\lambda)=\inf_{x\in\mathcal D} \left(f_0(x)+\lambda_1 f_1(x)+\cdots+\lambda_m f_m(x)\right),
\qquad \lambda\in\mathbb{R}^m,
\]

with domain restricted to $\lambda \succeq 0$.

We have seen several connections between $p^\star$ and $g$:

- \emph{Slater's condition and strong duality.} Slater's condition is: there exists $u \prec 0$ for which $p^\star(u) < \infty$. Strong duality (which then follows) is: $p^\star(0) = \sup_\lambda g(\lambda)$. (Note that we include the condition $\lambda \succeq 0$ in the domain of $g$.)

- \emph{A global inequality.} We have $p^\star(u) \geq p^\star(0) - \lambda^{\star T}u$, for any $u$, where $\lambda^\star$ maximizes $g$.

- \emph{Local sensitivity analysis.} If $p^\star$ is differentiable at $0$, then we have $\nabla p^\star(0) = -\lambda^\star$, where $\lambda^\star$ maximizes $g$.

In fact the two functions are closely related by conjugation. Show that, for $u \in \mathbf{int\ dom}\, p^\star$,

\[
p^\star(u) = (-g)^\star(-u),
\]

where $(-g)^\star$ denotes the convex conjugate of the function $-g$.

Hint. Consider the perturbed problem

\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{minimize}\quad & f_0(x) \\
\text{subject to}\quad & \tilde f_i(x) = f_i(x) - u_i \leq 0,\quad i=1,\ldots,m.
\end{aligned}
\]

Verify that Slater's condition holds for this problem when $u \in \mathbf{int\ dom}\, p^\star$.

Exercise 4.8
\emph{Exact penalty method for SDP.} Let $c\in\mathbf{R}^n$, and let $F_i\in\mathbf{S}^p$ for $i=0,1,\ldots,n$. Define the affine matrix-valued map
\[
F(x)=F_0+x_1F_1+\cdots+x_nF_n, \qquad x\in\mathbf{R}^n.
\]
Consider the primal-dual pair of semidefinite programs
\[
\begin{aligned}
& \text{(P)} \text{minimize} && c^T x \\
& \text{subject to} && F(x) \preceq 0,
\end{aligned}
\qquad
\begin{aligned}
& \text{(D)} \text{maximize} && \operatorname{tr}(F_0 Z) \\
& \text{subject to} && \operatorname{tr}(F_i Z) + c_i = 0,\quad i=1,\ldots,n \\
& && Z \succeq 0,
\end{aligned}
\]
where $\mathbf{S}^p$ denotes the space of $p\times p$ real symmetric matrices. Assume that (P) is feasible, and let $Z^\star$ be an optimal solution of (D). Show that every minimizer $x^\star\in\mathbf{R}^n$ of the unconstrained problem
\[
\text{minimize} \quad c^T x + M \max\{0,\lambda_{\max}(F(x))\},
\]
where $M > \operatorname{tr} Z^\star$, is an optimal solution of (P).

Exercise 4.10
Binary least-squares. Consider the nonconvex least-squares problem

\[
\begin{array}{ll}
\mbox{minimize} & \|Ax-b\|_2^2 \\
\mbox{subject to} & x_k^2 = 1,\quad k=1,\ldots,n,
\end{array}
\tag{10}
\]

where $A\in \mathbf{R}^{m\times n}$, $b\in \mathbf{R}^m$, and $\operatorname{rank}(A)=n$ (equivalently, $A^TA$ is nonsingular). In part (a), one obtains the SDP relaxation

\[
\begin{array}{ll}
\mbox{maximize} & -\operatorname{tr}(X A^TA)+2b^TAx-b^Tb \\
\mbox{subject to} & X_{ii}=1,\quad i=1,\ldots,n, \\
& \left[\begin{array}{cc}
X & x \\
x^T & 1
\end{array}\right] \succeq 0,
\end{array}
\]

with variables $X\in \mathbf{S}^n$ and $x\in \mathbf{R}^n$.

(b) Derive the Lagrange dual of this SDP and show that it is equivalent to

\[
\begin{array}{ll}
\mbox{minimize} & \operatorname{tr}(A^TAZ)-2b^TAz+b^Tb \\
\mbox{subject to} & \operatorname{diag}(Z)=1 \\
& \left[\begin{array}{cc}
Z & z \\
z^T & 1
\end{array}\right] \succeq 0,
\end{array}
\tag{11}
\]

where the variables are $Z\in \mathbf{S}^n$ and $z\in \mathbf{R}^n$, and $\operatorname{diag}(Z)=1$ means $Z_{ii}=1$ for $i=1,\ldots,n$.

Interpret problem (11) as a relaxation of problem (10). Show that if

\[
\operatorname{rank}\left(\left[\begin{array}{cc}
Z & z \\
z^T & 1
\end{array}\right]\right)=1
\tag{12}
\]

at an optimal solution of (11), then the relaxation is exact, i.e., the optimal values of problems (10) and (11) are equal, and the optimal solution $z$ of (11) is optimal for (10).

This suggests a heuristic for rounding the solution of the SDP (11) to a feasible solution of (10) when (12) does not hold. Let

\[
\left[\begin{array}{cc}
Z & z \\
z^T & 1
\end{array}\right]
=
\sum_{i=1}^{n+1}\lambda_i
\left[\begin{array}{c}
v_i \\
t_i
\end{array}\right]
\left[\begin{array}{c}
v_i \\
t_i
\end{array}\right]^T,
\]

be an eigenvalue decomposition, where $v_i \in \mathbf{R}^n$, $t_i \in \mathbf{R}$, and the eigenvalues satisfy $\lambda_1\geq \cdots \geq \lambda_{n+1}\geq 0$. Approximate the matrix by the rank-one matrix

\[
\left[\begin{array}{cc}
Z & z \\
z^T & 1
\end{array}\right]
\approx
\lambda_1
\left[\begin{array}{c}
v_1 \\
t_1
\end{array}\right]
\left[\begin{array}{c}
v_1 \\
t_1
\end{array}\right]^T,
\]

and take $x = \operatorname{sign}(v_1)$ as a candidate approximate solution of (10).

Binary least-squares. Consider the nonconvex least-squares problem

Monotone transformation of the objective. Consider the convex optimization problem

where $x \in \mathbf{R}^n$, the functions $f_i : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}$ for $i=0,1,\ldots,m$ are convex and differentiable, and the feasible set is nonempty. Let

for dual variables $\lambda \succeq 0$ and $\tilde\lambda \succeq 0$.

where $x\in\mathbf{R}^n$, the functions $f_0,f_1,\ldots,f_m$ are convex and differentiable on an open set containing the box $\{x\mid l\le x\le u\}$, and $l,u\in\mathbf{R}^n$ satisfy $l\le u$ componentwise.

Let $\mu \in \mathbf{R}_+^n$ be the Lagrange multipliers associated with the constraints $x_i \leq u_i$, and let $\nu \in \mathbf{R}_+^n$ be the Lagrange multipliers associated with the constraints $l_i \le x_i$ (equivalently, $l_i-x_i\le 0$). Thus the Lagrangian is

\[
L(x,\lambda,\mu,\nu)=f_0(x)+\sum_{i=1}^m \lambda_i f_i(x)+\mu^T(x-u)+\nu^T(l-x).
\]

In part (a), one constructs a dual feasible point by choosing $x$ and $\lambda\succeq 0$, and then selecting $\mu,\nu\succeq 0$ so that
\[
\nabla f_0(x)+\sum_{i=1}^m \lambda_i \nabla f_i(x)+\mu-\nu=0.
\]

(b) Apply this construction with
\[
x=\frac{l+u}{2},\qquad \lambda=0.
\]
From the resulting dual feasible point $(\lambda,\mu,\nu)$, obtain a lower bound on the optimal value $f^\star$. Show that this lower bound can be expressed as

\[
f^\star \geq f_0\!\left(\frac{l+u}{2}\right)-\left(\frac{u-l}{2}\right)^T \left|\nabla f_0\!\left(\frac{l+u}{2}\right)\right|,
\]

where $|\cdot|$ denotes componentwise absolute value. Can you prove this bound directly?

Exercise 4.14
Kantorovich inequality.

(b) Let $A\in \mathbf{S}_{++}^n$ be a real symmetric positive definite matrix with eigenvalues $\lambda_1\ge \lambda_2\ge \cdots \ge \lambda_n>0$. Using the result of part (a): for positive numbers $a_k>0$ and nonnegative weights $p_k\ge 0$ with $\sum_{k=1}^n p_k=1$, one has
\[
\left(\sum_{k=1}^n p_k a_k\right)^{1/2}\left(\sum_{k=1}^n p_k a_k^{-1}\right)^{1/2}
\le \frac12\left(\sqrt{\frac{\max_k a_k}{\min_k a_k}}+\sqrt{\frac{\min_k a_k}{\max_k a_k}}\right),

apply it with $a_k=\lambda_k$ to prove the Kantorovich inequality

\]
for every $u\in\mathbb{R}^n$ with $\|u\|_2=1$.

Exercise 4.24
Controlling a switched linear system via duality. Let $T \ge 1$ and $K \ge 1$ be integers. We consider a discrete-time dynamical system with state $x_t \in \mathbf{R}^n$, where $x_0 \in \mathbf{R}^n$ is given and fixed, and each mode matrix $A^{(i)} \in \mathbf{R}^{n \times n}$, $i=1,\ldots,K$, is given. The state propagates according to the recursion

\[
x_{t+1} = A_t x_t, \quad t=0,1,\ldots,T-1,
\]

where at each time $t$ the matrix $A_t$ must be chosen from the finite set $\mathcal{A}=\{A^{(1)},\ldots,A^{(K)}\}$. Let $f:\mathbf{R}^n\to \mathbf{R}\cup\{+\infty\}$ be a given proper function (you may assume $f$ is convex, though this is not necessary for this problem), and let $f^*$ denote its conjugate. The switched-linear control problem is

\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{minimize}\quad & \sum_{t=1}^T f(x_t) \\
\text{subject to}\quad & x_{t+1} = A^{(u_t)}x_t, \quad t=0,\ldots,T-1,
\end{aligned}
\]

with optimization variables $x_t \in \mathbf{R}^n$ for $t=1,\ldots,T$ and $u_t \in \{1,\ldots,K\}$ for $t=0,\ldots,T-1$.

Note that for any sequence $u_0,\ldots,u_{T-1} \in \{1,\ldots,K\}$, the recursion

\[
x_{t+1} = A^{(u_t)}x_t, \quad t=0,1,\ldots,T-1,
\]

determines a feasible trajectory. The switched-linear control problem is \emph{not} convex and is hard to solve globally. Instead, we consider a heuristic based on Lagrange duality.

(a) Find the dual of the switched-linear control problem explicitly in terms of $x_0$, $A^{(1)}, \ldots, A^{(K)}$, the function $f$, and its conjugate $f^*$. Your formulation cannot involve a number of constraints or objective terms that is exponential in $K$ or $T$ (including any minimization or maximization with an exponential number of terms).

Exercise 4.24
Controlling a switched linear system via duality. Consider a discrete-time dynamical system with state $x_t \in \mathbf{R}^n$ over a finite horizon $T\in\mathbf{N}$. The initial state $x_0\in\mathbf{R}^n$ is given and fixed. At each time $t=0,1,\ldots,T-1$, the state evolves according to
\[
x_{t+1}=A^{(u_t)}x_t,
\]
where the control $u_t$ must be chosen from $\{1,\ldots,K\}$, and $\mathcal A=\{A^{(1)},\ldots,A^{(K)}\}$ is a given finite set of matrices in $\mathbf{R}^{n\times n}$. Let $f:\mathbf{R}^n\to\mathbf{R}$ be a given function (you may assume $f$ is convex, although this is not needed here). The switched-linear control problem is
\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{minimize}\quad & \sum_{t=1}^T f(x_t) \\
\text{subject to}\quad & x_{t+1} = A^{(u_t)}x_t, \quad t=0,\ldots,T-1,
\end{aligned}
\]
with variables $x_1,\ldots,x_T\in\mathbf{R}^n$ and $u_0,\ldots,u_{T-1}\in\{1,\ldots,K\}$.

For dual variables $\nu_1,\ldots,\nu_T\in\mathbf{R}^n$ associated with the $T$ equality constraints
\[
x_t-A^{(u_{t-1})}x_{t-1}=0, \quad t=1,\ldots,T,
\]
the Lagrangian is
\[
L(x_1,\ldots,x_T,u_0,\ldots,u_{T-1},\nu_1,\ldots,
u_T)
=\sum_{t=1}^T f(x_t)+\sum_{t=1}^T \nu_t^T\bigl(x_t-A^{(u_{t-1})}x_{t-1}\bigr).
\]
The switched-linear control problem is generally nonconvex and hard to solve globally. Suppose now that $\nu_1^\star,\ldots,\nu_T^\star$ are optimal dual variables. Consider the heuristic of choosing the control sequence by minimizing the Lagrangian with these dual variables:
\[
(\tilde{u}_0,\ldots,\tilde{u}_{T-1}) \in
\arg\min_{u_0,\ldots,u_{T-1} \in \{1,\ldots,K\}}
\ \inf_{x_1,\ldots,x_T\in\mathbf{R}^n}
L(x_1,\ldots,x_T,u_0,\ldots,u_{T-1},\nu_1^\star,\ldots,\nu_T^\star).
\]
Given the optimal dual variables, show explicitly how to find $\tilde{u}_0,\ldots,\tilde{u}_{T-1}$.

Exercise 4.25
[Friedland and Karlin] Let $A\in\mathbf{R}^{n\times n}$ have strictly positive entries, and let $u,v\in\mathbf{R}^n$ be vectors with strictly positive components. Show that one can compute positive diagonal matrices $D_1,D_2\in\mathbf{R}^{n\times n}$ such that

\[
(D_1AD_2)u=u,\qquad (D_1AD_2)^Tv=v
\tag{23}
\]

by the following method. Define $\alpha_i=u_i v_i$ for $i=1,\ldots,n$, and consider the optimization problem

\[
\begin{array}{ll}
\mbox{minimize} & \displaystyle \prod_{i=1}^n \left(\sum_{j=1}^n A_{ij}x_j\right)^{\alpha_i} \\

with variable $x\in\mathbf{R}^n_{++}=\{x\in\mathbf{R}^n\mid x_i>0,\ i=1,\ldots,n\}$. Let $x$ be an optimal solution of (24), and define

Exercise 4.28
\emph{Robust least squares with polyhedral uncertainty.} Let $b_i\in\mathbf{R}$ for $i=1,\ldots,m$. Consider the robust least-squares problem

\[
\begin{array}{ll}
\mbox{minimize} & \displaystyle \sum_{i=1}^m \sup_{a_i\in P_i}(a_i^T x-b_i)^2
\end{array}
\]

with variable $x\in \mathbf{R}^n$. For each $i=1,\ldots,m$, the uncertainty set $P_i\subseteq\mathbf{R}^n$ is a nonempty bounded polyhedron defined by

\[
P_i=\{a_i\in \mathbf{R}^n\mid C_i a_i \preceq d_i\},
\]

where $C_i\in \mathbf{R}^{p_i\times n}$ and $d_i\in \mathbf{R}^{p_i}$. Introducing auxiliary variables $t_i\in\mathbf{R}$ satisfying
$t_i\geq \sup_{a_i\in P_i}|a_i^T x-b_i|$, we can equivalently write the problem as

\[
\begin{array}{ll}
\mbox{minimize} & \displaystyle \sum_{i=1}^m t_i^2 \\
\mbox{subject to} & \displaystyle \sup_{a_i\in P_i}\max\{a_i^T x-b_i,\ -a_i^T x+b_i\}\leq t_i,\quad i=1,\ldots,m.
\end{array}
\]

Formulate this optimization problem explicitly as a quadratic program (QP) in the variables $x$ and $t=(t_1,\ldots,t_m)$.
